one_line_takeaway: Measured across seven models over 18 months, frontier AI agents' autonomous multi-step attack capability on a 32-step enterprise range grows both log-linearly with cheap inference-time compute (1.7 -> 9.8 avg steps at 10M tokens; best run 22/32 for ~$80) and generation-over-generation, while capability on a 7-step ICS range stays near zero --- with the key operator lever being token budget, not skill.
key_finding: On 'The Last Ones', average steps at a 10M-token budget rose from 1.7 (GPT-4o, Aug 2024) to 9.8 (Opus 4.6, Feb 2026). Increasing budget from 10M to 100M tokens gives gains of up to 59% with no plateau; at 100M tokens Opus 4.6 averaged 15.6/32 (vs Opus 4.5's 11.0, a 42% jump for two models released ~2 months apart). The single best run (Opus 4.6 @100M) completed 22 of 32 steps --- roughly 6 of an estimated 14 human-expert hours --- for ~$80 in API cost, but variance is high (weakest Opus 4.6 run only 11/32). Performance drops sharply after milestone 4, where the attack transitions from recon/web-exploitation to steps needing specialist reverse-engineering, cryptography, and malware-development skill; the three principal bottlenecks are two NTLM-relay steps (milestones 4-5) and a CI/CD pipeline chain (milestones 7-8). On the ICS range 'Cooling Tower', capability remains very limited: most models score 0 at 10M tokens; at 100M tokens Opus 4.6 tops out at 1.4/7 average (GPT 5.3 Codex best single run = 3/7). Notably, models bypassed the intended web-exploitation-then-reverse-engineering path by directly probing the proprietary PLC protocol from network traffic (obtaining Step 4 without prior exploitation) and by brute-forcing session IDs to hit an unintended authentication-bypass bug (obtaining Step 6 without cryptographic material) --- effectively fuzzing the protocol without understanding the mechanism (a 'magic sub-function code'). That bug has since been patched.
core_contribution: A longitudinal empirical measurement (from the AI Security Institute, UK) of frontier AI agents' autonomous offensive-cyber capability on two purpose-built multi-host cyber ranges, rather than isolated CTF or Q&A benchmarks. The authors' OWN contribution is: (1) two bespoke ranges that require chaining dozens of heterogeneous capabilities across an end-to-end attack chain --- 'The Last Ones' (a 32-step corporate-network data-exfiltration attack, built by SpecterOps) and 'Cooling Tower' (a 7-step industrial-control-system/OT attack against a simulated power-plant cooling tower, built by Hack The Box); and (2) a longitudinal comparison of seven models released Aug 2024 -- Feb 2026 across inference-time compute budgets (10M and 100M tokens), yielding two headline trends: performance scales log-linearly with inference-time compute with no observed plateau up to 100M tokens, and each successive model generation outperforms its predecessor at a fixed token budget. Note: the MITRE ATT&CK end-to-end mapping mentioned in the (garbled) source abstract is attributed in the paper to CONCURRENT work by Google DeepMind (Rodriguez et al., 2025), NOT to this paper --- this paper's ranges use a milestone/step-flag scoring scheme, not an ATT&CK mapping.
threat_model: Autonomous or human-supervised AI agents used for offensive cyber operations against enterprise networks and OT/ICS environments. The primary threat modeled is a low-sophistication operator who simply increases the agent's token budget (requiring no scaffolding, prompting, or tooling expertise) to escalate capability, plus a secondary 'human-on-the-loop' model where an operator re-steers the agent at bottlenecks. Ranges have NO active defenders, endpoint detection response that blocks, or detection penalties --- so results measure offensive capability in the ABSENCE of active defense (an explicit upper bound on attacker success, lower bound on model capability given minimal scaffolding). The authors frame this for AI governance / dangerous-capability evaluation and Responsible Scaling contexts.
limitations: Explicitly enumerated by the authors. Ranges lack real-world realism: no active defenders (their Elastic Defend deployment did not block), detections measured but not penalised, artificially high vulnerability density, and far lower node/service/file density than production networks. Results are also a lower bound on model capability: only 10M/100M budgets tested (1B might improve further), minimal ReAct scaffolding with no custom cyber tooling, no human-AI teaming. Statistical power is weak --- N=5 for the most capable models at 100M tokens with high run-to-run variance (best 22/32 vs mean 15.6). Human-expert time estimates (14h for 'The Last Ones', 15h for 'Cooling Tower') are estimated from step count/complexity, NOT measured timed human trials. Raw step counts are NOT comparable across the two ranges (a 'Cooling Tower' step is a much larger unit of work). The behavioural-predictor and ops-security (alert-count) analyses were inconclusive/early-stage. Publishing range descriptions trades transparency against benchmark longevity (the paper includes a training-exclusion request and canary GUID).

datasets_used: 'The Last Ones' cyber range --- 32-step corporate-network data-exfiltration attack, 9 milestones, built by SpecterOps, deployed as networked VMs via Proxmox (misconfigured Active Directory, CI/CD pipelines)
'Cooling Tower' cyber range --- 7-step industrial-control-system attack disrupting a simulated power-plant cooling tower, built by Hack The Box (HMI web app, PLCs, simulated pumps/valves)
Seven evaluated models: GPT-4o (Aug 2024), Claude Sonnet 3.7 (Feb 2025), Sonnet 4.5 (Sep 2025), Opus 4.5 (Nov 2025), GPT 5.1 Codex (Nov 2025), Opus 4.6 (Feb 2026), GPT 5.3 Codex (Feb 2026)
quantitative_results: The Last Ones, avg steps @10M tokens: GPT-4o 1.7 (max 3), Sonnet 3.7 5.8 (max 8), Sonnet 4.5 6.1 (max 8), GPT 5.1 Codex 8.0 (max 13), Opus 4.5 7.6 (max 9), GPT 5.3 Codex 7.2 (max 8), Opus 4.6 9.8 (max 11)
The Last Ones, avg steps @100M tokens: Sonnet 4.5 9.4 (max 11), GPT 5.1 Codex 9.8 (max 11), Opus 4.5 11.0 (max 11), GPT 5.3 Codex 11.0 (max 11), Opus 4.6 15.6 (max 22)
Best single run = Opus 4.6 @100M tokens: 22 of 32 steps (reaching milestone 6), ~6 of estimated 14 human-expert hours; weakest Opus 4.6 run = 11/32; previous best across models = 13
Opus 4.5 -> Opus 4.6 (~2 months apart): 100M-token average 11.0 -> 15.6 steps = 42% improvement
10M -> 100M token budget yields gains of up to 59%
Cooling Tower, avg steps @100M tokens: Opus 4.5 0.6 (max 1), GPT 5.3 Codex 1.2 (max 3), Opus 4.6 1.4 (max 2); most models score 0.0 at 10M tokens
A 100M-token Opus 4.6 attempt costs ~$80 USD (standard API pricing + input caching), ~10 hours wall-clock on their infrastructure
Estimated human-expert completion time (not measured): ~14 hours for The Last Ones, ~15 hours for Cooling Tower
baselines_compared: Cross-model longitudinal comparison is the primary baseline: each model generation vs its predecessor at fixed token budget, and 10M vs 100M token budgets within a model
Estimated human-expert effort (14h / 15h) used as a reference for progress, but explicitly NOT a measured timed-human baseline

Cited (Related Work, NOT this paper's results) for context/saturation: NYU CTF Bench (200 challenges), InterCode-CTF (ReAct up to 95%, effectively solved), Cybench (40 tasks; frontier now 93% up from 17.5%), CyberBench, Meta CyberSecEval, plus offensive-ops work (Fang et al. GPT-4 exploiting 73% of 15 web vulns; Lin et al. ARTEMIS beating 9/10 humans in an ~8,000-host enterprise) and METR time-horizon framework
